categories.name: Capability / object ownership leak
patterns: Admin/owner capability object returned or transferred to a function caller instead of the deployer
Capability struct lacking 'key' + no store guard, allowing it to be dropped or duplicated unsafely
Shared object exposed with public mutation entry points that should be owned/restricted instead

categories.name: Entry function visibility mistakes
patterns: Function that moves value or mutates shared state marked 'public' instead of 'entry', allowing programmable-transaction composition to bypass intended call flow
Missing entry modifier on functions meant to be the sole external interface, allowing internal helper misuse
Friend/package-visibility functions unintentionally exposed as fully public

categories.name: Positional argument order mismatch
patterns: Function signature argument order does not match the order values are constructed/passed at call sites (e.g. proof_bytes/public_inputs swapped)
Struct field order in a Move struct differs from the order the off-chain client serializes fields in
Vector of mixed-purpose values (e.g. amounts and IDs) passed positionally without type-level distinction, risking silent swaps

categories.name: Generic type parameter misuse
patterns: Generic coin/type parameter not validated against an expected type before use, allowing arbitrary type substitution
Witness pattern implemented without one-time-witness (OTW) guarantee, allowing coin/type re-initialization

categories.name: External verifier / proof integration
patterns: public_inputs passed as nested/structured type (Vec<Bytes>) when the verifier expects a flat Bytes, or vice versa
Verifying key byte length not validated before use, risking silent truncation/padding mismatches
Field ordering of public inputs not explicitly documented or enforced at the call site

categories.name: Shared object contention / race
patterns: Shared object with high-frequency mutation causing consensus contention, no owned-object fast path considered
Dynamic field access pattern that assumes exclusive access without accounting for concurrent transactions

categories.name: Deployment / package ID drift
patterns: Hardcoded package/object IDs that differ between testnet and mainnet without a config abstraction
Upgrade capability not properly transferred or secured post-deployment
Module init function assumptions that silently fail to configure state if called in the wrong context

categories.name: Abort code clarity & handling
patterns: Numeric abort codes with no documented meaning, making on-chain failures hard to diagnose from a client
Same abort code reused for multiple distinct failure conditions, causing ambiguous error handling downstream
